source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $\angle BAC=90^\circ$, squares are constructed externally on sides $AB$, $CA$, $BC$. The area of square $ABIH$ is $25$, and the area of square $BDEC$ is $169$. What is the area of square $ACFG$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} In the right triangle $ABC$, $\angle BAC=90^\circ$, \\
$\therefore AB^2+AC^2=BC^2$, \\
$\because$ the area of the square $ABIH$ is $25$, and the area of the square $BDEC$ is $169$, \\
$\therefore AB^2=25, BC^2=169$, \\
$\therefore AC^2=BC^2-AB^2=169-25=144$, \\
$\therefore$ the area of the square $ACFG$ is $AC^2=\boxed{144}$.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says 'As shown in the figure'
requires_image=true and text_dominant=false in the input
the geometry setup is largely spelled out in text (right triangle plus three external squares), so the image may help but may not be strictly necessary to compute the answer
multi_step: solution requires mapping square areas to side-length squares (AB^2=25, BC^2=169)
then applying the Pythagorean relation in the right triangle and subtracting to obtain AC^2
this is a short but genuine multi-step geometric/algebraic reasoning chain
verifiability: target output is a single numeric area, which is easy to check
answer_text provides an explicit derivation using AB^2+AC^2=BC^2 and 169-25=144
registry_passed=true, question and answer are both present, and there are no listed asset issues
